1:
The narrative turns from flight to refuge, and the witnesses attend first to the place, then to the people who gather, and last to the faith that governs David's every move. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the scene with a traveller's eye: the great valley of Elah, the highway from Philistia to Hebron, is honeycombed with natural caves, 'roomy and dry,' still used by shepherds for themselves and their flocks; David chose one of these fastnesses, and Ellicott finds there was 'ample room for the 400 refugees,' Stanley even calling the Adullam cavern 'a subterranean palace, with vast columnar halls and arched chambers.' The name, he adds, was borrowed from the old Canaanite city of Adullam (Joshua 15:35). Adam Clarke fixes the site ten miles eastward of Eleutheropolis in the tribe of Judah, and sketches the chapter's grim course in advance — the flight, the sheltering of the parents, and the slaughter at Nob. John Gill only notes that David came 'glad of the deliverance' from Achish's court.

The Puritan Matthew Henry turns the cave into a parable. David, who might have served his country as judge or general, is 'shut up in a cave, and thrown by as a vessel in which there was no pleasure'; we must not think it strange, Henry says, if 'shining lights be thus eclipsed and hidden under a bushel,' and he hears in the Old-Testament worthies who 'wandered in deserts, in dens and caves of the earth' (Heb. 11:38) an echo of this very hour. It was here, he remembers, that David penned Psalm 142, the prayer of a man who complained that 'no man would know him.' To him his brethren and all his father's house flocked — 'a brother is born for adversity' — Joab and Abishai among the first of the mighty men (1 Chron. 11:15).

Henry lingers most tenderly on the parents lodged in Moab: 'the first thing he does is to find them a quiet habitation, whatever became of himself,' and he bids children learn to requite their parents (1 Tim. 5:4), for 'though David's father and mother forsook him, God did not' (Ps. 27:10). Above all he marks the humble faith of the refrain, 'till I know what God will do for me' — David expects his deliverance 'not from his own arts, or arms, or merits,' but from the wisdom and goodness of God, and takes the prophet Gad for his guide. So the witnesses close where the verse leaves them: the steps of a good man ordered by the Lord, and the Son of David ready to receive distressed souls who will make him their captain.
2:
The witnesses fasten on the strange muster-roll of David's first army — the distressed, the indebted, and the discontented — and agree that grace made a disciplined force out of unpromising material. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the social background. Keil and Delitzsch describe a 'large number of malcontents,' men 'bitter of soul,' dissatisfied with Saul's government, who gathered to about four hundred and soon swelled to six hundred (1 Sam. 23:13), and who 'ripened into heroic men under the command of David during his long flight,' their bravest names preserved in 1 Chron. 11:15. Charles Ellicott, citing Ewald, sees a country 'becoming more and more melancholy under Saul,' driving men to seek a better leader; David did not send the refugees away but 'organised them into a military force' to guard the southern frontier from the plundering nomads, winning a popularity that 'in after years stood him in good stead.' On the debtors, Ellicott traces the misery to the collapse of the Mosaic protections against avarice (Exod. 22:25; Lev. 25:36) under Saul's capricious rule.

The Wesleyan and reformed voices are anxious to clear the men's character. Adam Clarke concedes that some may at first have hoped to profit by plunder and revenge, 'but if this were their design they were greatly disappointed, for David never made any improper use of them'; they are 'never found plundering or murdering,' but appear 'under good discipline,' so that David 'succeeded in civilizing them, and making profitable to the state those who were before unprofitable.' John Wesley reads the debtors kindly as 'poor debtors, whom the creditors were obliged to spare' (Exod. 22:25), whose persons were with David though their goods remained liable; and on the captaincy he insists David 'did not maintain any injustice or wickedness,' but 'instructed and obliged them to the practice of all justice and honesty.' John Gill, briefest of all, glosses the distress as 'straitened circumstances, through the oppression of men, through poverty, and afflictive providences.'

The one small difference among them is whether these were 'exceptionable characters' or merely the oppressed; Clarke protests that we need not 'strain the words of the original' to make them criminals. Yet the schools converge on the pastoral wonder of the scene: God gathers the broken, the bankrupt, and the embittered under the anointed but rejected king, and forms of them the nucleus of a kingdom — a picture, the older readers cannot help adding, of the Son of David receiving all who come to him.
3:
David's errand to the king of Moab draws the witnesses to three things: the place, the kinship that made the refuge natural, and the modest faith of his closing words. The grammatical-historical commentators locate Mizpeh. Keil and Delitzsch take it literally as 'a watch-tower or mountain height commanding a very extensive prospect,' probably a fastness on the mountains of Abarim or Pisgah (Deut. 34:1), easily reached from Bethlehem by crossing the Jordan near the Dead Sea, and they infer from Saul's earlier war on Moab (1 Sam. 14:47) why David sought asylum beyond his enemy's reach. Charles Ellicott notes this Mizpeh is 'mentioned nowhere else,' perhaps the Zophim of Num. 23:14, and grounds the whole appeal in blood: 'David evidently sought hospitality among his kin in Moab,' since Jesse was the grandson of Ruth the Moabitess. John Gill adds that the name marks a fortified place, where David 'might think himself safer, though in an enemy's country, than in the land of Israel.'

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke reads David's motive practically: he 'could not trust his parents within the reach of Saul,' and the fatigues of a military life were unsuitable for them, so he begs the Moabite king for shelter — a king who, being 'one of Saul's enemies, would be the more ready to oblige' the son of Jesse. John Wesley and Matthew Henry both hear in 'till I know what God will do for me' the accent of a man who 'had entirely cast himself upon God, and committed his way to him, trusting not in his own arts or arms, but in the wisdom, power and goodness of God.'

Ellicott alone lingers on a delicate detail of speech: David, addressing an idolater, says 'God' — 'Elohim' — not 'Jehovah,' and Ellicott takes this 'out of deep reverence,' since 'an idolater had nothing to do with the awful name by which the Eternal was known to His covenant people.' It is a small window into David's piety, that even in exile and negotiation he guards the covenant Name. The voices do not contend; together they show a fugitive who provides first for his aged parents, leans on kinship and prudence, and rests the whole uncertain venture on what God, not his own contrivance, will do for him.
4:
This short verse of settlement — the parents left with the king of Moab 'all the while that David was in the hold' — is read by the witnesses chiefly as a question of the meaning of 'the hold' and of the length of the shelter. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative forward, noting how the settling of the parents opens directly into Gad's counsel to depart and into Saul's fatal council at Gibeah (v.6), where 'what follows took place in a solemn conclave of all the servants of Saul' gathered to deliberate. Charles Ellicott ties the term down: this 'hold' is 'identical with the hold of 1 Sam. 22:5, from which Gad the prophet directs David to depart,' and it was 'most likely in the Land of Moab.'

The Wesleyan John Wesley reads the singular more broadly. 'Hold,' he says, stands for 'holds; the singular number being put for the plural, as is frequent' — that is, 'as long as David was forced to go from place to place, and from hold to hold, to secure himself.' On this view the parents' stay in Moab was not confined to the brief period at Mizpeh or Adullam 'which was but a little while,' but covered the whole wandering season; for, Wesley observes, 'it concerned David to secure his father, and he did doubtless secure him for all that time.' John Gill, following the Targum, simply reads that David 'caused them to remain before' the Moabite king, having obtained his leave.

The interest of the verse, then, is quietly chronological and pastoral. Where Ellicott pins 'the hold' to one Moabite stronghold, Wesley widens it to the succession of refuges David was driven through; but neither turns it into a dispute. Both honour the same instinct in David — that a hunted man's first duty was to see his aged father and mother safely lodged before he exposed himself again to the frights and fatigues of the chase. The parents disappear from the story here, kept in safety across the Jordan, while the scene shifts back to the land of Judah and to the gathering storm of Saul's jealousy that the next verses unfold.
5:
The prophet Gad steps into the story, and the witnesses weigh both the man and his counsel, and puzzle a little over the word 'forest.' The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives Gad his due at length: from this point 'throughout the life and reign of David' the prophet 'occupied a marked place,' named the king's seer (2 Sam. 24:11), reckoned among the compilers of David's acts with Samuel and Nathan (1 Chron. 29:29), and later the man bold enough to reprove the king for numbering the people and to bring the choice of three evils. Ellicott ventures the 'hardly baseless conjecture' that Gad was 'a direct messenger from the old prophet Samuel to his loved pupil David,' perhaps a fellow student in the Naioth at Ramah — one of the rare few in the prophetic schools who received the true 'Divine influence.' John Gill agrees that Gad was 'one of the company of the prophets, over whom Samuel was president,' whether he accompanied David into exile or was now sent to him.

On the counsel itself the voices are one. Ellicott reads the 'wise advice, suggested by a Divine influence,' as bidding David 'not to estrange himself from his own country and people by remaining in a foreign land,' for 'there was work for him and his followers to do in that distracted, harassed land.' The Wesleyan John Wesley presses the further purpose: David must 'shew' himself in Judah to 'publicly put in his claim to the kingdom after Saul's death,' that his friends 'may be invited and encouraged to appear on his behalf'; and beneath it all, Wesley adds, 'God would exercise David's faith, and wisdom, and courage, and so prepare him for the kingdom.'

The one genuine crux is the destination — 'the forest of Hareth.' Ellicott records that the Septuagint and Josephus read 'the city of Hareth,' and that Lieutenant Conder, finding 'no trace of forest' on the edge of the Hebron chain where Kharas now stands, 'believes the LXX text the true one'; yet Dean Payne Smith holds that the 'thickets' still growing there abundantly are exactly what the Hebrew 'yar' signifies. The witnesses leave the map slightly open, but the movement is plain: at a prophet's word David turns from Moab back to the perilous soil of his own tribe, and so obediently walks toward the throne by the road of danger.
6:
The scene shifts to Saul's court, and the witnesses read the tableau — the king seated under his tree, spear in hand, his servants about him — as the very portrait of a jealous tyranny hardening toward murder. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the picture. Charles Ellicott corrects the setting with care: not 'a grove in a high place' but 'under the tamarisk tree on the height' in Gibeah of Saul, the king's own city; leaning 'as was his wont in peace as in war, upon his tall spear,' surrounded by captains chosen 'with one exception from his own tribe of Benjamin' — the exception the Edomite Doeg. Ellicott calls it 'one of the earliest councils we have any definite account of in the world's history,' a king 'complaining of the treason' of an exile and 'bewailing the want of fidelity of his son.' Adam Clarke reads the spear as 'the ensign of power,' stuck in the ground to mark the general's tent, and takes the standing servants for a council of war.

The Puritan Matthew Henry names the sitting for what it is — 'this bloody court of inquisition' — and dissects Saul's jealousy. The king 'really sought David's life, and therefore pretended that David sought his life'; he charges Jonathan as a confederate, though 'a league of friendship there was between David and Jonathan, but no conspiracy in any evil thing.' Henry catches the psychology exactly: 'See the nature of a jealous malice, and its pitiful arts to extort discoveries of things that are not.' Saul 'looked upon all about him as his enemies because they did not say just as he said,' and 'if a ruler hearken to lies, all his servants are wicked' (Prov. 29:12) — that is, seem so in his eyes. John Gill notes only that the report of David 'in arms, and at the head of a number of men' possessed Saul with 'fears and jealousies of his people, and all about him.'

Where Ellicott reads statecraft — a king who has estranged the nation by heaping honours on his own tribe, and who will pay for it on Gilboa (1 Sam. 31) — Henry reads the soul, a mind 'blinded and hardened.' They do not disagree; the one shows the policy and the other the poison, and both prepare us to grieve at what Saul's spear, that 'sceptre' of force, is about to command against the priests of the Lord.
7:
Saul's opening appeal — 'Hear now, ye Benjamites' — becomes for the witnesses a self-portrait of a king ruling by favouritism and fear. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in it 'a fair specimen of Saul's manner of ruling in his later years,' and marvels at the candour: the king has 'gradually given all the posts of honour and dignity to men of his own tribe and family, or to strangers like Doeg,' and now appeals to that very partiality as the ground of their loyalty. 'Who but a Benjamite,' Ellicott paraphrases, 'would only honour Benjamites?' Such a sovereign 'had surely forfeited his kingdom,' and the fruit of the policy showed in 'the thin array he was able in his hour of bitter need to muster' on Gilboa (1 Sam. 31). Keil and Delitzsch supply the bribe behind the rhetoric: will the son of Jesse give you 'fields and vineyards' and make you 'captains of thousands'? — the argument being that David, of Judah, could have no such patronage to spend on Benjamin.

The Wesleyan John Wesley reads the appeal in the same key, and spells out its unspoken threat: 'Ye Benjamites — you that are of my own tribe and kindred, from whom David designs to translate the kingdom to another tribe.' Will he, Saul insinuates, 'distribute profits and preferments among you Benjamites, as I have done? Will he not rather prefer those of his own tribe before you?' The whole speech works on tribal jealousy and self-interest, binding the servants to the king by their purses rather than their consciences. John Gill notes simply that Saul 'took this opportunity of addressing them' on the report of David at the head of his men.

The voices do not divide; they agree that the verse lays Saul's misgovernment bare. He does not summon his men to justice or to God, but to the calculation of gain and loss, and he assumes they must think as basely as he does. In appealing to the worst in his followers Saul betrays the ruin of his own kingship — a throne held by force and favour, already emptied of the loyalty that flows from love, and ripe for the tragedy the following verses record.
8:
Saul's accusation — that 'all of you have conspired against me' and none disclosed his son's league with David — is read by the witnesses as the raving of a mind wholly mastered by suspicion. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds the tragedy in its reach: 'the unhappy, jealous spirit had obtained such complete mastery over the unhappy king that now he suspected even the chosen men of his own tribe' — his 'tried favourites, the men of his own house, even his gallant son.' Ellicott thinks the pointed language about a covenant shows Saul 'had learned something of what passed between Jonathan and David' at the New Moon feast, 'too pointed and marked to refer only to the well-known ancient friendship'; and he hears in 'there is none of you that is sorry for me' words 'strangely real and pathetic,' the cry of one 'tormented by an evil spirit, ever whispering doubt and jealous thoughts into the poor diseased mind.'

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke lays bare the logical impossibility of the charge. Saul 'conjectured that Jonathan had made a league with David to dethrone him, and accuses them of disloyalty for not making the discovery of this unnatural treason'; but 'it was impossible for any of them to show what did not exist, no such league having ever been made between David and Jonathan.' John Wesley compresses the whole into a maxim: 'See the nature of jealousy, and its arts of wheedling to extort discoveries of things that are not.' John Gill catches the perverse reasoning by which silence itself became guilt — 'since they did not discover to him what he supposed they knew, and showed no concern for his circumstances, he interpreted this a conspiracy against him.'

The witnesses are united and unusually moved. There is no debate here, only a shared diagnosis: a king demanding that his servants confess a plot that never was, and counting their honest ignorance as treason. Clarke's point is decisive — no one can produce evidence of a non-existent league — yet the pathos Ellicott hears keeps the reader from mere contempt. Saul is not simply wicked but wretched, a man whose jealousy has made every friend an enemy and emptied his court of the truth, and whose next words will turn from complaint to the shedding of innocent blood.
9:
Doeg the Edomite breaks the servants' silence, and the witnesses examine both his office and his motive with a common distaste. The grammatical-historical commentators settle who he was. Keil and Delitzsch note that he is here styled 'the superintendent of Saul's servants,' apparently 'invested with the office of marshal of the court,' and could not 'refrain from yielding to this appeal, and telling Saul what he had seen when staying at Nob' — that Ahimelech had inquired of God for David and armed him with Goliath's sword (1 Sam. 21). Adam Clarke, comparing the earlier notice that Doeg was 'chiefest of the herdmen,' reckons him 'the king's equerry or groom,' set over the royal beasts. Charles Ellicott observes why his testimony bit so deep: 'the Edomite's witness had the more effect on Saul because he related no hearsay evidence, but what he had absolutely seen.'

The moral judgement is unanimous and sharp. John Gill sees Doeg 'full of enmity to David, and willing to curry favour with Saul, and eager of further preferment,' so that 'being more forward than the rest of his servants,' he 'prevented them and spoke first.' Ellicott quotes the old remark of Sebastian Schmid with approval: 'Far better did Saul's other servants who kept silence.' The contrast is the point — the very reticence of the Benjamite courtiers, whom Saul had just abused as disloyal, shows more conscience than the eagerness of the foreign informer.

The witnesses do not divide; they agree that Doeg's speech, though every fact in it was true, was the act of a self-serving and cruel man, timed for advancement and aimed at blood. His Edomite descent is quietly marked by more than one — the outsider, over the herds, who alone of all Saul's court will supply the accusation the king wants and, presently, the hand to execute it. Where the native servants held their peace, the ambitious stranger comes forward; and the tragedy of Nob is set in motion not by evidence newly discovered but by malice newly given its opportunity. It is worth marking, too, that Doeg had witnessed the meeting at Nob long before (1 Sam. 21) and had held his peace until the moment of profit came; the informer chooses his hour, and Saul's public complaint gave him the opening he had been waiting for.
10:
The heart of Doeg's report — that Ahimelech 'inquired of the Lord' for David, fed him, and gave him Goliath's sword — opens the chapter's one real exegetical dispute: did the priest in fact consult the oracle? The grammatical-historical commentators divide. Keil and Delitzsch accept it: though the earlier narrative (1 Sam. 21) 'has no reference to his inquiring of God, it certainly took place, as Ahimelech does not disclaim it' when charged (v.15). Charles Ellicott is doubtful: 'this is by no means certain; nothing was said about the Urim and Thummim being brought out,' and he suggests Doeg 'was misled by the fact of the high priest's going into the sanctuary, where the ephod was, to fetch the sword of Goliath,' which 'was laid up behind the ephod.'

The Wesleyan Adam Clarke thinks the consulting probable but unrecorded, 'as David would most naturally wish to know where to direct his steps in this very important crisis.' John Wesley, however, presses Doeg's dishonesty: David 'chargeth him with the sin of lying' (Ps. 52:3), and Wesley holds him guilty of 'concealing part of the truth' — the artifice by which David made Ahimelech 'believe he was then going upon the king's business, so that the service he did to David was designed in honour to Saul.' The reformed John Gill weighs both sides: some take the inquiry 'to be a lie of Doeg's,' but Gill thinks 'it is not at all improbable' David asked it and Ahimelech did it; and he records the sharp Jewish reading that Doeg meant the charge to prove 'treason both against David and Ahimelech,' since 'inquiry by Urim and Thummim was not made for a private person, but for a king' — David thereby claiming, and the priest owning, a royalty not yet his.

The witnesses converge on the deeper point even where the fact is uncertain: Doeg's three-fold report is true to the letter yet false in its spirit. Whether or not the oracle was consulted, the priest acted in innocence, believing David to be on the king's errand; and Doeg's careful selection of damning facts, stripped of that exculpating context, is the very art of the false witness who lies most effectively by telling half the truth.
11:
Saul summons not Ahimelech alone but 'all his father's house, the priests that were in Nob,' and the witnesses read the sweep of the summons as itself an omen of the slaughter to come. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott catches the sinister implication at once: 'this sending for all the priestly house to Gibeah, when alone Ahimelech was to blame — if blame there was — looks as though Saul and Doeg had determined upon the wholesale massacre which followed.' The order goes beyond any pretence of justice; a king seeking merely to examine one supposed offender would not have called up the whole company of the priests, and their obedient coming, 'all of them,' shows a body with no consciousness of guilt and no thought of danger.

The Wesleyan John Wesley adds the note that turns the scene toward prophecy fulfilled: these were 'the priests of the house of Eli, which God had threatened to cut off' (1 Sam. 2:31). Ahimelech, descended from Eli, stands at the head of a house already under an ancient sentence; and so the reader, watching the priests file in at Saul's word, sees two things at once — the tyrant's premeditated cruelty and the long-deferred judgement of God moving, through that cruelty, to its appointed end. John Gill records only the bare fact, that Saul 'sent messengers to him, and summoned him to appear before him.'

The voices do not contend; between them they hold the verse's double aspect in a single frame. On the surface it is an act of royal summons, formally correct, courteously obeyed. Beneath it, Ellicott discerns a plot already laid and Wesley a word of God long since spoken (1 Sam. 2:31) now bending the wickedness of Saul to serve the righteousness of God. The innocent priests come trustingly to the height at Gibeah, and the stage is set for the confrontation and the sentence that the next verses record. That they came 'all of them,' without a single flight or excuse, is itself a proof of their good conscience: men who had plotted with David would have fled to his standard rather than answer Saul's summons, but these had nothing to hide, and so walked, unsuspecting, into the trap.
12:
The trial opens, and the witnesses fasten on the contempt packed into Saul's address — 'Hear now, thou son of Ahitub' — and on the calm dignity of the priest's reply. The reformed John Gill reads the king's phrasing as a deliberate slight: Saul utters 'the charge exhibited against him in contempt of him,' for 'he does not so much as call him by his name, nor give him the title of his office, as high priest' — and this to a man who was 'the second person in the kingdom, and to whose office a few years ago the civil government was annexed.' The refusal of name and title is not carelessness but insult; Saul strips the high priest of his honour before he strips him of his life.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the exchange in its full shape, quoting the indictment and the defence together: Saul demands, 'Why have ye conspired against me, thou and the son of Jesse, in that thou hast given him bread, and a sword, and hast inquired of God for him?' — and Ahimelech answers, 'And who is so faithful among all thy servants as David, which is the king's son in law, and goeth at thy bidding, and is honourable in thine house?' Keil and Delitzsch note that the priest speaks 'both calmly and worthily,' pleading a faithfulness 'proved, attested,' as the same word is used of Moses (Num. 12:7), and reading David's standing as 'having access to thy private audience, and honoured in thy house.'

The two readings are complementary rather than opposed. Gill exposes the malice in Saul's opening words; Keil and Delitzsch display the transparent innocence of the answer they provoke. The priest to whom the king denies even his name replies with the one thing a false accuser cannot bear — the plain truth that David was, to all the world's knowledge and his own, the most loyal servant the king possessed. Ahimelech's 'Here I am, my lord' is the answer of a clear conscience that expects no wrong and imagines none, and it throws the coming sentence into the sharper relief of manifest injustice.
13:
Saul spells out the charge — that Ahimelech conspired with the son of Jesse 'in that thou hast given him bread, and a sword, and hast inquired of God for him' — and the witnesses weigh which of the three counts weighed heaviest in the king's mind. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott singles out the oracle: 'this using of the Urim and Thummim for David is again repeated by the king,' and 'it seems in Saul's eyes to have been the gravest of the charges imputed to the high priest by Doeg.' The proof, Ellicott observes, is that Ahimelech in his defence 'specially recurs to this point with peculiar insistence,' answering only 'Did I then begin to inquire of God for him?' (v.15). Of the three acts — bread, sword, oracle — it is the last that Saul construes as treason, for to seek God's guidance for David was, in the king's jealous logic, to treat the fugitive as the true anointed of the Lord.

The reformed John Gill fastens on the injustice of the framing itself — 'why have ye conspired against me' — which assumes the very guilt it pretends to examine. Saul does not ask whether Ahimelech helped David, a fact freely admitted, but why he 'conspired,' loading an innocent kindness with the weight of a plot. The word turns hospitality into sedition and a routine consultation into an act of war against the crown.

The two voices, few as they are, agree on the shape of the thing. Saul has already fixed the verdict and now reads the evidence backward to fit it: the bread that any priest would give a king's son-in-law, the sword that lay in the sanctuary, the inquiry made in good faith — all are recast as the machinery of rebellion. Ellicott's insight that the oracle was the sharpest count, and Gill's that the whole indictment presumes the guilt, meet in one conclusion: this is not a trial seeking truth but a sentence seeking a pretext, and Ahimelech's answer will expose it as such. The verse thus prepares the reader to feel the weight of what follows: a king who frames kindness as conspiracy has already, in the very wording of his question, passed the judgement he pretends still to be weighing, and the priest's reply in the next verse will meet a mind closed against it.
14:
Ahimelech's defence — 'And who is so faithful among all thy servants as David?' — is read by the witnesses as the honest word of a man who knew only David's public reputation and reasoned from it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott finds the words 'quiet and dignified,' speaking 'no doubt the general sentiments of the people respecting David': what the guardian of the sanctuary had done, 'he had done as a matter of course for one so closely related to the king — for one, too, ever loyal and devoted as David had ever proved himself.' The priest's plea rests entirely on David's known standing — the king's son-in-law, who 'goeth at thy bidding, and is honourable in thine house' — and so it is unanswerable on its own terms.

Adam Clarke adds a philological caution that has divided readers. The word rendered 'faithful,' he notes, is 'neeman,' which 'is probably the name of an officer' rather than a mere adjective (compare Num. 12:7) — as though David held a recognised place of trust at court, not simply a good character. The Wesleyan John Wesley guards the priest's sincerity from a subtler angle: Ahimelech 'doth not determine the difference between Saul and David, nor affirm what David now was,' but 'only declared what David formerly had been, and what he was still, for anything he knew to the contrary.' The priest, that is, testifies to David's tried fidelity without pretending to judge the present quarrel of which he knew nothing.

The reformed John Gill simply frames the answer as spoken 'first with respect to David, and then with regard to himself.' The witnesses do not really debate; whether 'neeman' names an office (Clarke) or only a quality, all agree that Ahimelech's defence is transparently true and precisely the reason his help was innocent. He had honoured David as the most faithful of Saul's servants because that is what all Israel, and Saul himself until lately, believed him to be. The tragedy of the verse is that a plea so manifestly just falls on a king who has already resolved not to hear it.
15:
Ahimelech's key sentence — 'Did I then begin to inquire of God for him?' — is the verse on which the witnesses do their closest work, for its very rendering is disputed. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays out three readings. The English Version makes it an indignant question implying long practice: David 'had on many previous occasions received through him Divine directions.' Abarbanel, whom Ellicott finally prefers, renders it a statement: 'That was the first day that I enquired of God for him, and I did not know that it was displeasing to thee.' A third takes it as a bare denial, 'Did I inquire? — No.' Ellicott ties the priest's whole plea to a single misunderstanding: 'the reason why Ahimelech allowed the sacred Urim to be consulted was that he supposed David was come, as he represented, on a mission direct from King Saul.' He even cites the Talmud (Treatise Yoma) that 'the king, or the chief of the legislative administration, who alone had the privilege of consulting the Urim, stood facing the priest' — which, if the right was royal, sharpens Ahimelech's innocence, since he took David to be on the king's errand.

Adam Clarke reads the same words in the same generous direction: Ahimelech means 'that his inquiring now for David was no new thing, having often done so before, and without ever being informed it was either wrong in itself, or displeasing to the king'; nor 'is it likely that Ahimelech knew of any disagreement between Saul and David,' for 'he knew him to be the king's son-in-law, and he treated him as such.' The reformed John Gill notes Kimchi's alternative to the interrogative — 'that day I began to inquire of God for him' — and voices the priest's heart: 'I did not know he fled from thee; had I known it, I would never have done it, and as it is the first time it shall be the last.'

The debate over grammar leaves the substance untouched. Whether Ahimelech protests a long custom of inquiring for David or confesses a first and innocent consultation, every witness draws the same conclusion — 'thy servant knew nothing of all this' — and the priest stands acquitted at the bar of conscience even as the king prepares to condemn him.
16:
Against a defence so transparent — 'thy servant knew nothing of all this' — Saul answers with the sentence of death: 'Thou shalt surely die, Ahimelech, thou, and all thy father's house.' The reformed John Gill lets the verse stand almost bare, and reads it as the naked exercise of tyranny: the king 'pronounces the sentence himself, without taking the opinion and advice of others, or further time,' and this, Gill insists, 'was an act of arbitrary power, and upon an innocent person, which was an act of great injustice.' Every clause of his brief note names a distinct wrong — that Saul is at once accuser and judge; that he consults no counsel and grants no interval; that the condemned is guiltless; and that the whole proceeding is not justice but its opposite.

The words of the sentence themselves press the injustice further than Gill needs to say. Ahimelech has just pleaded, calmly and truly, that he knew of no quarrel between the king and his son-in-law, and had done for David only what any priest would do for a trusted servant of the crown. To that plea the king opposes no evidence and no argument, only the doom — and a doom that reaches past the one man Doeg accused to 'all thy father's house,' the whole priesthood of Nob, against whom nothing whatever had been alleged. The verdict does not weigh the defence; it simply overrides it with the bare will of the king.

Gill's single sentence, then, opens onto the moral centre of the chapter. Here is a ruler who has cast off the fear of God, sitting in his own cause, condemning the innocent and their kindred with them, and pronouncing sentence in the heat of jealousy rather than the deliberation of law. The reader who has followed Ahimelech's honest answer feels the full arbitrariness of the words; and the sentence, monstrous as it is against one high priest, becomes more monstrous still as it widens to an entire house, and moves at once toward the execution that the following verses will record. Gill's restraint is itself instructive: where the sin is so plain, the commentator need not argue it, but only name the four marks of a lawless judgement — one man judging his own cause, no counsel taken, no time allowed, and the innocent condemned with their kindred — and let the verse's own words carry the horror.
17:
Saul turns the sentence into an order — 'Turn, and slay the priests of the Lord' — and the witnesses fix on two things: who the appointed executioners were, and the honourable refusal that met the command. The grammatical-historical commentators identify the 'footmen.' Charles Ellicott renders the word literally 'runners,' the royal guard or 'lictors' who 'ran by the royal chariot as an escort' and could 'maintain a great speed for a long time' — the very class Samuel had warned the people a king would take to 'run before his chariot' (1 Sam. 8:11; compare Elijah outrunning Ahab, 1 Kings 18:46). Keil and Delitzsch weigh the terrible phrasing, noting how 'the priests of the Lord' is repeated to throw 'the crime of the bloodthirsty Saul' into its true light: 'the very dress which the priests wore, as the consecrated servants of Jehovah, ought to have made them shrink from the commission of such a murder.' John Gill records the Jewish tradition that the runners were Abner and Amasa, though he thinks them rather 'princes' than footmen.

The refusal draws the warmest comment. Adam Clarke honours it plainly: the servants 'dared to disobey the commands of the king in a case of such injustice, inhumanity, and irreligion.' Ellicott quotes Wordsworth to the same effect — 'thus they were more faithful to Saul than if they had obeyed his order, which was against the commandment of the Lord, whose servant the king was no less than they.' Here the guardsmen, whom Saul had gathered to do his will, prove the one point of conscience in the court: they will run at the king's word, but they will not lift a hand against the anointed priests of God.

The witnesses stand together, and the moment gathers a quiet irony. Saul had accused his servants of disloyalty for keeping silence (v.8); now their true loyalty shows precisely in disobedience, a loyalty to God above the king that the king himself has forfeited. Their refusal both convicts Saul and, for a moment, stays his hand — until he finds in the foreigner Doeg an instrument as pitiless as his own command.
18:
The deed the guards refused, Doeg performs, and the witnesses dwell on the executioner, the number of the slain, and the desecration the priestly robe makes plain. The reformed John Gill asks bitterly who was fitter for the work than 'the false accuser of them, and false witness against them,' now turned executioner. Keil and Delitzsch underline the sacrilege: the repeated mention of the 'linen ephod' and 'priests of Jehovah' shows that 'the very dress which the priests wore ought to have made them shrink' from the murder, and Doeg 'at once performed the bloody deed.'

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan voices weigh the count of the dead. Adam Clarke notes the variants — the Hebrew's 'fourscore and five,' the Septuagint's three hundred and five, Josephus's three hundred and eighty-five — and proposes a reconciliation: 'probably the eighty-five were priests; the three hundred, the families of the priests,' the larger figure being 'the whole population of Nob.' The men who fell 'did wear a linen ephod,' that is, 'persons who did actually administer, or had a right to administer, in sacred things.' Charles Ellicott pictures them coming from Nob to Gibeah 'clad in their official costume, out of respect to the king who sent for them,' so that 'the murderous deed assumes a still more awful character when we recollect who were the victims — the priests of the living God, clad in their white ministering robes.'

On the executioner the witnesses are unsparing. Clarke calls Doeg 'a ruthless Edomite, capable of any species of iniquity'; John Wesley reads the man's nation as the key — the Edomite is named 'to wipe off the stain of this butchery from the Israelitish nation, and to show why he was so ready to do it, because he was one of that nation which had an implacable hatred against all Israelites, and against the priests of the Lord.' Ellicott adds that the most ancient Jewish writings 'execrated' Doeg 'above any other of the famous wicked men' of Scripture. The voices do not divide; whether they count eighty-five or three hundred, they agree that the linen ephod turns a massacre into a sacrilege, and that the foreign hand which the native guards would not lend has written Saul's ruin in the blood of God's priests.
19:
The slaughter widens from the priests to their whole city, and the witnesses read the devastation of Nob — 'men and women, children and sucklings, and oxen, and asses, and sheep' — as Saul's deepest crime and the measure of his fall. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch see the horror in the totality: 'not content with even this revenge, Saul had the whole city of Nob destroyed, like a city that was laid under the ban' (Deut. 13:13), 'so completely did Saul identify his private revenge with the cause of Jehovah, that he avenged a supposed conspiracy against his own person as treason against Jehovah the God-king.' Charles Ellicott makes the same point — the king treated 'the innocent city' as a place 'under the ban, as though it had been polluted with idolatry,' when 'the only crime of Nob' was that its chief citizen 'had shown kindness to David.'

Ellicott then ventures the chapter's boldest historical conjecture. The three-year famine 'for Saul and his bloody house, because he slew the Gibeonites' (2 Sam. 21:1) records a massacre 'nowhere specially related' elsewhere; and Ellicott asks whether it was not this very destruction of Nob, since the Gibeonites were 'hewers of wood and drawers of water for the Tabernacle' (Josh. 9:21) and may well have perished when the priestly city fell. He quotes Dean Payne Smith: it was left 'to the anointed king of Israel, who had himself settled the priests at Nob, to perpetrate an act unparalleled in Jewish history for its barbarity.'

The reformed and Wesleyan voices name the spiritual cause. John Gill reports that Saul 'sent men thither to slay all the inhabitants.' John Wesley pronounces the verdict of the whole company: 'In all the life of Saul, there is no wickedness to be compared to this. He appears now to be wholly under the power of that evil spirit which had long tormented him.' The witnesses do not contend; between them they hold the twofold truth the chapter presses — that Saul, unrighteous in the deed, has drawn down on himself a barbarity without parallel, and that the destruction breaks upon every pious Israelite as a calamity that makes them 'wish a thousand times they had been content with the government of Samuel.'
20:
Out of the massacre one priest escapes — Abiathar, son of Ahimelech — and the witnesses read his flight to David as both a providence and the beginning of a long history. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark him 'the only one of the whole body of priests who escaped this bloody death,' who 'fled after David' and told him of 'the barbarous vengeance which Saul had taken upon the priests of the Lord.' They translate David's self-reproach precisely: 'I knew that day that the Edomite Doeg was there... I am the cause of all the souls of thy father's house,' and read David's promise as pledging protection — 'thou art safe with me,' the word for keeping meaning 'protected, well kept.' Charles Ellicott fills in Abiathar's after-story: he 'became after his father's death high priest,' remained David's 'faithful friend' through the reign, but at the last 'espoused the side of Adonijah' and was 'deposed by Solomon and sent into banishment to Anathoth' (1 Kings 2:26); he thinks Abiathar likely 'remained behind to perform the necessary functions in the sanctuary' and so escaped.

The Puritan Matthew Henry draws out the pastoral and typical sense. Abiathar, 'before Doeg and his bloodhounds came to Nob, had intelligence of the danger'; and 'whither should he go but to David?' — for 'let those that suffer for the Son of David commit the keeping of their souls to him' (1 Pet. 4:19). Henry weighs David's grief that he was 'accessory' to the calamity — 'it is a great trouble to a good man to find himself in any way an occasion of the calamities of the church and ministry' — and remembers that David now penned Psalm 52, painting Doeg as 'false and deceitful,' yet reckoning himself 'a green olive-tree in the house of God.' John Gill notes that Abiathar took 'the ephod, with the Urim and Thummim,' in his flight, and so carried the oracle to David's camp.

The witnesses do not divide; they gather the verse into one lesson. Saul's fury cannot defeat God's purpose: the very sword that emptied the sanctuary drives its surviving priest, and the ephod with him, into the hands of the coming king, so that David, an outlaw with a prophet already at his side, now has a priest also — 'a happy omen of his success,' as Henry says, and a sign that the true worship of Israel has passed, with Abiathar, from Saul's reach to David's protection.
21:
The verse is a single stroke of narrative — 'Abiathar shewed David that Saul had slain the Lord's priests' — and the witnesses read it as the bringing of dreadful news to the one man who would feel it most. The reformed John Gill imagines the moment of disclosure: the tidings were such 'as perhaps he had not as yet heard, though tidings of such a nature generally fly swiftly,' and Abiathar 'had a sorrowful shocking account to give, and which was so to David.' The escaped priest carries to the outlaw camp not rumour but eyewitness certainty of the slaughter, and Gill lets the pathos rest on the phrase 'the Lord's priests' — for it is the ministers of Israel's own God who have been cut down.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the report in its immediate sequel, quoting the exchange whole: Abiathar's news, then David's reply, 'I knew it that day, when Doeg the Edomite was there, that he would surely tell Saul: I have occasioned the death of all the persons of thy father's house,' and finally the promise, 'Abide thou with me, fear not.' By holding the three verses together they show that the announcement is not left hanging; it draws from David at once both a confession and a pledge, so that the horror of the message issues immediately in David's owning of his share in it and his taking of the survivor under his care.

The two voices, spare as the verse is, agree on its function. It is the moment the crime reaches the ears for which it was, in a sense, committed — for Saul's whole rage was against David — and Nob's blood now cries out in David's own camp. The messenger who escaped the sword becomes the witness whose word will move David to grief, to self-reproach, and to the protection of Abiathar that closes the chapter. What Saul meant as a stroke against David has placed in David's hands the last priest of Nob and the whole terrible knowledge of the deed. Gill's stress on how swiftly such tidings fly only deepens the point: the news that Saul would least wish David to hear is the very news that reaches him first, borne by the one man the slaughter failed to silence.
22:
David's answer to Abiathar — 'I knew it that day, when Doeg the Edomite was there... I have occasioned the death of all the persons of thy father's house' — is read by the witnesses as a rare and moving act of self-accusation. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the plain sense: 'when I saw Doeg there, I suspected he would make the matter known to Saul,' and 'I have been the innocent cause of their destruction.' The word 'innocent' is exact — David had done no wrong, yet he takes the guilt of the outcome upon himself, tracing the whole calamity back to his own presence at Nob and the danger he had failed to forestall.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the confession against the long background of enmity between David and Doeg. He follows the Talmudic tradition that 'a bitter enmity existed' between them, the Edomite 'brought up with Saul in the family of Kish' and 'jealous for his master with the passionate jealousy we so often find in old servants,' who 'would share and probably fan his royal master's envy and fear' of the 'brilliant young hero.' So when David, 'flying for his life, met Doeg at the Sanctuary of Nob, he was seized with grave misgivings as to what would happen'; and now he 'reproaches himself with having in Doeg's presence exposed the hapless priest Ahimelech to Saul's furious anger.' The reformed John Gill reads the memory inwardly — 'I knew it that day' means 'he thought in his mind at that time' — so that David's foreboding at Nob is now confirmed as true prophecy of the ruin that followed.

The witnesses do not divide; they draw one lesson from David's grief. Here is greatness of soul in a hunted man: he does not excuse himself, does not shift the blame to Saul or Doeg where much of it justly lay, but owns himself the occasion of a slaughter he never intended and could not have prevented without foresight he lacked. It is, as the Puritan reading of the neighbouring verse had it, a great trouble to a good man to find himself any way the cause of evil to others; and David's honest 'I have occasioned' stands in deliberate contrast to Saul's whole refusal, throughout the chapter, to own any guilt at all.
23:
The chapter closes on David's word of refuge to the trembling priest — 'Abide thou with me, fear not... but with me thou shalt be in safeguard' — and the witnesses read it as both a pledge of protection and a bond of shared danger. The reformed John Gill pictures Abiathar's state: he 'appeared to be in a fright, which is not to be wondered at, as not knowing what to do, and where to go and provide for his safety'; and David, 'to allay his fears, and make him easy, invites and encourages him to stay,' bidding him 'not be afraid of Saul, nor any other.' The fugitive who has lost father, brethren, and city is taken in by the one man whom the same king hunts.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws out the logic of David's comfort: 'he that seeketh my life seeketh thy life' means 'the enmity of Saul is directed against thee as well as against me, and thou canst have no safety but in being closely attached to me.' The very fact that binds them in peril binds them in security — a common enemy makes a common cause. And David adds a resolve of conscience: 'I will defend thee even at the risk of my own life,' a thing, Clarke says, 'he was bound in duty and conscience to do,' since it was his own presence at Nob that had brought the priesthood to ruin.

The two voices do not divide; they close the narrative on a note that answers all its cruelty. Over against Saul, who slew the priests of the Lord and burned their city, stands David, who gathers the last of them under his own protection at the hazard of his life. The outlaw, hunted from hold to hold, becomes the sanctuary of the sanctuary's survivor; and the chapter that opened with David sheltering in a cave ends with David himself become a shelter. So the reader is left with the two kings side by side — the reigning one shedding innocent blood, the coming one guarding it — and with the quiet assurance that those who suffer for the Lord's anointed shall, with him, be kept safe.
